content:
# Genesis Chapter 22 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 12r–12v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_022_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_022_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0021 (page 17) and https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0022 (page 18)

---

## The Binding of Isaac

**1** After all this, God put Abraham to the test.

"Abraham!" he said.

"Here I am," Abraham answered.

**2** God said, "Take your son — your only son, Isaac, whom you love — and go to the land of Moriah. There I want you to offer him as a burnt offering on one of the mountains I will show you."

---

**3** Abraham got up early the next morning. He saddled his donkey, took two of his servants with him and his son Isaac, and split wood for the burnt offering. Then he set out for the place God had described.

**4** On the third day Abraham looked up and saw the place in the distance. **5** He said to his servants, "Stay here with the donkey. The boy and I will go on ahead. We will worship, and then we will come back to you."

**6** Abraham loaded the wood for the burnt offering onto Isaac's shoulders. He himself carried the fire and the knife. And the two of them walked on together.

**7** Isaac spoke up. "Father?" he said.

"Yes, my son?" Abraham answered.

"We have the fire and the wood," Isaac said, "but where is the lamb for the burnt offering?"

**8** "God will provide the lamb for the burnt offering, my son," Abraham said.

And the two of them walked on together.

---

**9** When they reached the place God had told him about, Abraham built an altar and arranged the wood on it. Then he bound his son Isaac and laid him on top of the wood on the altar. **10** He reached out his hand and took the knife to kill his son.

**11** But the angel of the LORD called out to him from heaven: "Abraham! Abraham!"

"Here I am!" he said.

**12** "Do not lay a hand on the boy," the angel said. "Do not do anything to him. Now I know that you truly fear God — you did not withhold from me your son, your only son."

**13** Abraham looked up, and there — caught in a thicket by its horns — was a ram. He went over and took the ram and offered it as a burnt offering in place of his son. **14** Abraham named that place "The LORD will provide." And to this day people say, "On the mountain of the LORD it will be provided."

---

**15** The angel of the LORD called out to Abraham from heaven a second time **16** and said, "I swear by myself — this is the LORD's own declaration — because you have done this, because you did not withhold your only son, **17** I will bless you without measure and multiply your descendants beyond counting — like the stars in the sky and the sand on the seashore. Your offspring will take hold of the gateway of their enemies. **18** Through your offspring, every nation on earth will find blessing — because you obeyed me."

**19** Then Abraham returned to his servants. They got up and went back together to Beer-sheba, and Abraham settled in Beer-sheba.

---

**20** Some time later, Abraham received word: "Your brother Nahor's wife Milcah has also borne him children — **21** Uz the firstborn, his brother Buz, and Kemuel the father of Aram, **22** Chesed, Hazo, Pildash, Jidlaph, and Bethuel." **23** Bethuel became the father of Rebekah. These eight children Milcah bore to Nahor, Abraham's brother. **24** His concubine, whose name was Reumah, also bore children — Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, and Maacah.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Genesis 22 — the chapter that silences commentary

There is a reason the rabbis called this *HaAqedah* — the Binding. Not "the sacrifice," not "the test of Abraham." The Binding. Because the act at the center of the story is the act of binding — the father's hands working the rope around the son, the son laid on the wood, the knife drawn — and what that means is left suspended, like the moment itself, for every reader who has ever come to it.

I will try to explain the translation choices. But first: this chapter does not need explanation. It needs to be read.

---

### Verse 1 — "After all this"

**וַיְהִי אַחַר הַדְּבָרִים הָאֵלֶּה** — "And it happened after these things."

The Hebrew phrase *achar haddevarim ha'elleh* is a standard transitional formula in Genesis, used to shift scene or time. But here it lands with particular weight. *After all this.* After the long wait for Isaac. After the laughter and the joy of his birth. After the painful expulsion of Ishmael, which Abraham resisted. After the treaty at Beer-sheba, where chapter 21 ended in contentment — Abraham planting a tree, calling on the Everlasting God, settled in the land. *After all this.* The phrase signals that everything comfortable has just ended.

The word translated "test" — **נִסָּה** (*nissah*) — is a Piel perfect of *nasa*, meaning to test, to prove, to put to the trial. Critically, this is not the word for *temptation* (*nasha*, to lure into sin). The narrator tells us, from the very first line, what this is: a test. Not a divine cruelty. Not an ambush. A test. The reader knows something Abraham does not know: God does not intend for Isaac to die. This narrative irony is the source of almost all the chapter's tension. We know; Abraham does not know; and yet we watch in horror as he proceeds as though he does not know what we know.

The LXX Vaticanus renders *nissah* as **ἐπείραζεν** (*epeirazen*) — "was testing" — which is the imperfect, suggesting ongoing action: God *was putting to the test* Abraham. The imperfect in Greek carries the sense of a process, not a single moment. The Vaticanus text is readable in the manuscript before me and aligns closely with the Leningrad Hebrew here.

---

### Verse 2 — the fourfold identification

**קַח-נָא אֶת-בִּנְךָ אֶת-יְחִידְךָ אֲשֶׁר-אָהַבְתָּ אֶת-יִצְחָק** — "Take, please, your son, your only son, whom you love, Isaac."

This is one of the most devastating sentences in the Hebrew Bible, and its devastation is entirely a matter of structure. God identifies Isaac in four successive layers, each one more particular and more precious than the last:

1. *your son* — בִּנְךָ (*binkha*)
2. *your only son* — יְחִידְךָ (*yechidkha*): the unique one, the singular one, the one-of-a-kind
3. *whom you love* — אֲשֶׁר-אָהַבְתָּ: the one you love
4. *Isaac* — יִצְחָק: his name, last of all

The effect is like a series of tightening hands around the heart. Each addition makes the command harder. The name comes last — after the reader has already understood, through three qualifications, exactly what is being asked. *Your son. Your only son. The one you love.* Then: *Isaac.*

The particle **נָא** (*na'*) — "please" — is often untranslated or rendered "now," but it is a particle of entreaty, of polite request. It is stunning that God uses it here. Not *Go and take your son* as a command. *Take, please, your son.* The politeness is not weakness. It is the opposite: it makes the command more terrible, not less, because it is spoken without coercion. Abraham is being asked, not ordered. This is the test.

---

### Verse 3 — the early morning

**וַיַּשְׁכֵּם אַבְרָהָם בַּבֹּקֶר** — "Abraham got up early in the morning."

This is the same phrase used in Genesis 21:14, when Abraham got up early to send Hagar and Ishmael away. There too, the early rising followed a painful divine command. There is no delay. No prolonged night of anguish described, no record of a sleepless wrestling — the text gives us none of that. It only says he got up early and went. The obedience is immediate and the reader must stand in the silence of that gap, imagining what the night before must have been.

Kierkegaard spent an entire book, *Fear and Trembling*, inside that gap. The text does not.

---

### Verse 4 — "on the third day"

**בַּיּוֹם הַשְּׁלִישִׁי** — "on the third day."

Three days of walking toward Moriah. Three days with Isaac. Three days knowing what awaited at the end of the journey. The three-day journey will echo through later tradition at frequencies impossible to mishear: Jonah in the fish, Jesus in the tomb. Whether those are intended echoes or retrospective readings is a question the text declines to answer. But the detail lodged itself in the memory of all who came after.

---

### Verse 5 — "we will come back to you"

**נָשׁוּבָה אֲלֵיכֶם** — "we will return to you."

Both verbs in this sentence — "we will go," "we will worship," "we will return" — are first person plural. *We* will go. *We* will worship. *We* will come back. Abraham tells his servants that both he and Isaac will return.

Is he lying? Is he expecting a miracle? Is he expressing a faith so total that he can speak of return even while carrying the knife? The rabbis debated. The Christian tradition, following Hebrews 11:17–19, read it as faith that God could raise Isaac from the dead — Abraham was, in effect, already acting in the logic of resurrection. The text will not explain it. What it gives us is the plural: *we* will return. And at verse 19, after everything, Abraham does return — to his servants. The boy is with him. They go back together. The plural held.

---

### Verses 6–8 — the walk together

**וַיֵּלְכוּ שְׁנֵיהֶם יַחְדָּו** — "and the two of them walked together."

This phrase occurs twice: once at the end of verse 6 and once at the end of verse 8. It is the hinge of the passage. Father and son, walking toward the altar, the wood on the son's shoulders, the fire and knife in the father's hand. Then the terrible innocent question: *Where is the lamb?* Then the answer: *God will provide.* Then, again: *and the two of them walked together.*

The repetition is the genius of the text. After the question — after the knife has, in some sense, been named between them — they still walk together. The father's answer does not resolve the tension. But it does not sever the walking. They continue forward, side by side.

The answer Abraham gives — **אֱלֹהִים יִרְאֶה-לוֹ הַשֶּׂה** — "God will see to it, the lamb" — is a grammatical pivot point. The verb *yir'eh* means "will see," and the *lo* could be read as "for himself" (God will provide *for himself* the lamb) or as a general "to it" (God will see *to* the lamb). The same verb is the root of the name given to the place in verse 14: *Yhwh-yireh*, "the LORD will see/provide." The answer anticipates the naming; the naming fulfills the answer. The whole chapter is latent in these words: God will see. God will provide. On the mountain of the LORD, it will be seen.

I have rendered it "God will provide the lamb" — which is how this phrase has shaped Christian and Jewish liturgical memory — while noting that the Hebrew word *see* and the English word *provide* do not fully overlap. To provide is what you do with your hands; to see is what you do with your eyes and your care and your presence. Abraham's answer carries both: God will *notice*, God will *act*.

---

### Verses 9–10 — the binding

**וַיַּעֲקֹד אֶת-יִצְחָק בְּנוֹ** — "and he bound Isaac his son."

The verb **עָקַד** (*aqad*) — to bind, to tie — appears only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. This is its one and only occurrence. Wherever else binding is described in the OT, different words are used. This word is unique to this moment. Isaac — *aqedah*, the binding. Because of this single verse, this rare and singular word, the episode carries the name it has carried for three thousand years.

The text says nothing about Isaac's response. He is bound and placed on the altar and the knife is raised — and we are told nothing of what Isaac said or thought or did. The silence around him is as terrible as anything that is said. He received the wood on his shoulders in verse 6 the way a victim receives a cross. He asked the question that split the narrative in verse 7. And then he disappears into the father's action. He is simply there, bound, on the altar.

The Vaticanus LXX renders this scene with the same economy as the Hebrew, offering no expansion or commentary on Isaac's experience. The restraint is itself the statement.

---

### Verse 11 — the interruption

**וַיִּקְרָא אֵלָיו מַלְאַךְ יְהוָה מִן-הַשָּׁמַיִם** — "and the angel of the LORD called to him from the heavens."

The name **Abraham! Abraham!** — the doubled call — is the only other doubled-name address in Genesis. The first was **Jacob! Jacob!** (46:2). The doubling signals urgency, intimacy, the full attention of the one called. It is also, perhaps, a sound. The text gives us the moment the silence breaks: the knife raised, the name called twice across the silence, and Abraham answering — as he answered at the very beginning of the chapter — *here I am*.

**הִנֵּנִי** (*hineni*) — "here I am." He said it when God first called in verse 1. He says it again when the angel calls in verse 11. The word has not changed. Abraham is still present, still available, still here. It is the same man in both moments, responding with the same word. The test is over.

---

### Verse 12 — "now I know"

**עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי כִּי-יְרֵא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה** — "now I know that you fear God."

This verse has generated a vast theological debate: does God *learn* something here? Is the omniscient God claiming new knowledge? Classical theologians argued that God knew all along but the "now I know" signals the moment the knowledge *becomes manifest*, *becomes publicly demonstrated*, *becomes real in time*. The test was not for God's information; it was for Abraham's formation — and perhaps for the witness of heaven and earth.

The word **יָרֵא** (*yare'*) — "fear" — is the same root as the possible etymology of Moriah (*ha-Moriyyah*): "the place of fearing," or "the place where the LORD is seen/feared." The chapter is saturated in this root: to see, to provide, to fear, to be seen. God saw to the lamb. On the mount of the LORD it will be seen. Now the angel knows that Abraham fears God. The vocabulary forms a net, and the chapter is caught in it.

---

### Verse 13 — the ram

**וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה-אַיִל אַחַר נֶאֱחַז בַּסְּבַךְ בְּקַרְנָיו** — "He looked up, and behold — a ram, behind, caught in the thicket by its horns."

The word **אַחַר** (*achar*) — "after," "behind" — is the same word used in verse 1: *achar haddevarim*, "after these things." Abraham looked and saw a ram *behind* him — something that had been there, perhaps, all along, behind the horizon of his attention, while he was focused forward on the unbearable thing in front of him. The provision was already there. It was behind him.

This is theologically momentous. The ram is not conjured on the spot. It was there before the knife was raised. This is what it means that "the LORD will provide" — not that God will scramble at the last moment, but that the provision exists in the world already, waiting for the moment of need to be revealed. The seeing (*yir'eh*) is not invention. It is *discovery*: what was already there, now seen.

---

### Verse 14 — the naming

**וַיִּקְרָא אַבְרָהָם שֵׁם-הַמָּקוֹם הַהוּא יְהוָה יִרְאֶה** — "Abraham called the name of that place: YHWH-Yireh."

The name is a monument. Abraham has done what his ancestors did across Genesis — he has named a place for what happened there, or for what he learned there. *YHWH-Yireh*: the LORD will see. The LORD will provide. The LORD sees to it.

The second half of the verse is among the most grammatically interesting in the chapter: **אֲשֶׁר יֵאָמֵר הַיּוֹם בְּהַר יְהוָה יֵרָאֶה** — "as it is said to this day: on the mountain of the LORD it will be seen/provided." The verb here — **יֵרָאֶה** (*yera'eh*) — is a Niphal, passive: "it will be seen," or "it will be provided," or "he will appear." The passive form opens every direction at once. The LORD himself will appear. The provision will appear. Something will be seen. On this mountain — which tradition identified with Jerusalem, with the Temple Mount — *seeing* and *providing* and *appearing* collapse into a single moment.

The name has outlasted every dynasty. Every time the Temple was built or rebuilt, every time a worshiper ascended the mount, this name was there underneath it.

---

### Verses 15–18 — the oath

**כִּי בִי נִשְׁבַּעְתִּי** — "by myself I have sworn."

God swears by God's own name. There is no higher authority. This is the covenant renewed and deepened — the blessing of Abraham, the multiplication of descendants like stars and sand, the possession of the gate of enemies, the blessing of all nations through Abraham's offspring. These promises were made before (Genesis 12, 15, 17). But now they are sworn with an oath, anchored not in a covenant ceremony but in Abraham's obedience. The oath comes *because* Abraham did not withhold his son.

**בְּזַרְעֲךָ** (*bézar'akha*) — "in/through your offspring." This is the word that Paul will later seize in Galatians 3:16 and argue refers to a single descendant, translating the singular *zera'* (*seed*). The grammar of a collective singular — *seed* can mean one grain or the whole harvest — is both the occasion for Paul's argument and its limitation. The Hebrew *zera'* is regularly used for collective descendants (all of Isaac's line) and also for a single anticipated descendant. Both readings are alive in the text.

**וְהִתְבָּרְכוּ בְזַרְעֲךָ כֹּל גּוֹיֵי הָאָרֶץ** — "all the nations of the earth will bless themselves / will be blessed through your offspring." The verb **הִתְבָּרְכוּ** (*hitbareku*) is a Hithpael — reflexive or reciprocal — which introduces genuine ambiguity: do the nations *bless themselves* by invoking Abraham's name ("may I be blessed like Abraham"), or are they *passively blessed* through his line? Both traditions of interpretation have ancient standing. The LXX Vaticanus, visible in the manuscript before me, renders this **ἐνευλογηθήσονται** — a future passive: "they *will be blessed*." The Greek tradition reads it as passive blessing. The Hebrew holds the ambiguity. I have translated "every nation on earth will find blessing" — which is slightly softer on the passive/active distinction, letting the finding of blessing carry the weight.

---

### Verse 19 — the return

**וַיָּשָׁב אַבְרָהָם אֶל-נְעָרָיו** — "Abraham returned to his servants."

And they went back together to Beer-sheba.

The chapter does not give us an emotional conclusion. There is no scene of father and son embracing. No description of what either of them said or felt on the way down. The narrative, which had been so taut and so close, suddenly widens and goes quiet. *Abraham returned to his servants. They got up and went to Beer-sheba.* The silence after the storm is almost worse than the storm. What do you say to each other on the walk down from a mountain like that?

Genesis will not tell us.

What it will tell us, three chapters later, is that when Sarah dies, Isaac is living in a region to the south, and Abraham comes from Beer-sheba to mourn her. They are not living together. Whether the Aqedah fractured something between them is not said. But the text remembers the distance.

---

### Verses 20–24 — Nahor's family: the arrival of Rebekah

The sudden shift to genealogy at verse 20 is a classic Genesis technique: the great narrative concludes, and the next story quietly begins to grow in the background. Abraham receives news about his brother Nahor's family back in the land he came from. Among the children of Bethuel, one name is listed at the end of verse 23: **רִבְקָה** (*Rivqah*) — Rebekah.

This is her first appearance in the Bible.

She is introduced with no drama and no description. She is one name in a list. But the reader of Genesis knows — from the narrative momentum and from the fact that Isaac is now conspicuously not yet married — that this name matters. The next chapter will bury Sarah; the one after that will send a servant back to this family to find Isaac a wife. The genealogy is not a postscript. It is a preview.

The structure of Genesis 22 ends, then, on a note of continuation. The test is over. The oath is renewed. Beer-sheba is home. And in a far country, a girl named Rebekah has been born — the mother of the next generation of the covenant.

---

### The Aqedah as a whole: what the text does

The Aqedah has generated more commentary, more theological argument, more literary meditation, more devotional reflection than almost any other passage in the Hebrew scriptures. This translation cannot contain what interpreters have done with it. But a few things are worth naming.

**The silence of the text is intentional.** We are not told how Abraham felt when God spoke. We are not told what Isaac thought or said once he understood what was happening. We are not told what the servants made of the three-day journey or the strange return. We are not told what the ram thought of any of it. The narration gives us actions and words with almost no access to interiority, except through the crack of one question: *Where is the lamb?* That question, asked by a child, carries everything the narrator refuses to give us directly. It is the wound in the text.

**The chapter is about the return of the son.** Genesis 21 ended with a son sent away — Ishmael, driven into the desert. Genesis 22 begins with a command to sacrifice the remaining son. Both chapters press against the same terror: the loss of the child. Genesis 21 resolves with Ishmael alive and blessed. Genesis 22 resolves with Isaac alive and the covenant deepened. The sequence is not accidental. The God who provides for the expelled son provides also for the bound son. The pattern is: the child will not be lost.

**"Your only son" carries echoes forward.** The phrase **אֶת-יְחִידְךָ** — "your only son" — appears in verse 2 and is repeated, deliberately, in verse 12 and verse 16. The New Testament authors heard these words and applied them to the crucifixion: *God so loved the world that he gave his only Son* (John 3:16). Whether this was the text's intention, it was unquestionably the tradition's reception. The binding of Isaac became, in Christian reading, the type and shadow of the cross — with this crucial difference: the angel stopped Abraham's hand, and no angel stopped the other.

**The chapter asks one question.** Not "will Abraham obey?" — the narrative makes clear from verse 3 that he will. The question is: *what is this God doing?* The God who gave the son now demands the son. The God who said "it is through Isaac that your family line will carry your name" (21:12) now says "take Isaac and sacrifice him." These two statements are in direct contradiction, and Abraham walks three days into that contradiction without resolving it. He resolves it, at last, not with an argument but with a step — placing his foot on the mountain, piling the stones, laying the wood, binding his son, raising the knife.

And then the name: *Yhwh-Yireh.* The LORD sees. The LORD provides. On the mountain of the LORD, it will be seen.

The chapter is its own answer.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 12r–12v, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 22 begins in the right column of folio 12r (page 001 for this chapter), continuing the text immediately following Genesis 21:34, and continues through folio 12v (page 002), where the chapter concludes at verse 24 and Genesis 23 begins. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 17–18 (pa_0021–pa_0022; accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0021 and /0022). The Vaticanus LXX is visible in the manuscript images and is largely in agreement with the Leningrad Codex Hebrew throughout Genesis 22. Notable divergence: the LXX Vaticanus renders the verb of verse 18 (*hitbareku*) as a future passive (*ἐνευλογηθήσονται* — "they will be blessed"), resolving the Hebrew Hithpael's ambiguity in the direction of passive blessing. The DSS text 4Q225 (Pseudo-Jubilees) contains an interpretive retelling of the Aqedah and is not used as a textual source here, though its existence is noted.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
